title: One Man's Meat: How High Prices Are Swapping Donkey for Horse in China
content:
A recent local news report revealed that soaring prices for donkey meat are forcing some restaurants in China to make surprising substitutions. In the northern Henan province, soup joints have begun secretly switching to horse meat to maintain their profit margins. Some establishments in Zhengzhou have even added the character for "horse" beside "donkey" on their shopfronts. This indicates that merchants expect the controversial meat substitution to become a permanent change. Prices for genuine donkey meat have jumped by as much as fifty percent since last year, now costing roughly twice as much as beef.

This dramatic price increase occurred after food safety crackdowns abruptly exposed a shrinking supply base that had been declining for decades. Online commentators from northern China have bemoaned the soaring costs of their regional specialty. Meanwhile, southern Chinese citizens have expressed confusion over why anyone would want to eat donkey in the first place. As China completes its transition from an agricultural society to an industrial one, this traditional working-class staple has slowly turned into a luxury. Even for specialized demands, China's massive scale means its consumption habits can shake ecologies and markets thousands of miles away.

Donkey has long been a cherished regional specialty in northern China, where the cooler weather is highly conducive to raising the animals. There is even a local saying that claims while heaven has dragon meat, earth has donkey meat. Unfortunately, the high demand and low supply have led to a network of illicit workshops producing counterfeit products. In response, authorities in multiple provinces have seized hundreds of tons of chemically altered horseflesh sold as donkey meat. Unless the government offers subsidies to incentivize farmers, genuine donkey meat is likely to remain a rare and expensive luxury product.
